questions.text: Which of the following are the most prominent part of cell membranes in living organisms?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, terpenoids are not the most prominent part of cell membranes in living organisms. The most prominent part of cell membranes in living organisms are phospholipids. Phospholipids are a type of lipid that is made up of two fatty acids bonded to a glycerol molecule. They have a hydrophilic head and a hydrophobic tail. This allows them to form a bilayer that separates the inside of the cell from the outside environment. Terpenoids are a type of lipid that is made up of isoprene units. They are found in many different places in living organisms, including cell membranes. However, they are not the most prominent part of cell membranes.

Waxes are also lipids, but they are not as prominent in cell membranes. Waxes are made up of fatty acids and long-chain alcohols. They are hydrophobic, meaning that they do not dissolve in water. Waxes are found in the outer layer of cell membranes, where they help to protect the cells from water loss and from harmful chemicals.

Phospholipids are the most prominent part of cell membranes in living organisms. They make up about 50% of the mass of most cell membranes, and they are essential for the structure and function of the membrane.Phospholipids are amphipathic molecules, which means that they have both hydrophilic (water-loving) and hydrophobic (water-fearing) parts. The hydrophilic head of the phospholipid is made up of a phosphate group and a small group of other atoms. The hydrophobic tails of the phospholipid are made up of two fatty acids. The hydrophilic heads of the phospholipids are attracted to water, while the hydrophobic tails are repelled by water. This causes the phospholipids to spontaneously form bilayers in water. The hydrophilic heads of the phospholipids line up on the outside of the bilayer, where they are exposed to water. The hydrophobic tails of the phospholipids line up on the inside of the bilayer, where they are protected from water.

Acylglycerols are made up of a glycerol backbone and three fatty acid chains. They are nonpolar, meaning that they are not soluble in water. Acylglycerols are found in cell membranes, but they are not as abundant as phospholipids. They are more commonly found in the form of triglycerides, which are used for energy storage.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct. Phospholipids are the most prominent part of cell membranes in living organisms. questions.text: Type of RNA that is used to start process of replication in prokaryotic cell:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: tRNA (transfer RNA) is a small RNA molecule that plays a key role in protein synthesis. It is responsible for carrying amino acids to the ribosome, where they are assembled into proteins. Each tRNA molecule is specific for a single amino acid, and it has an anticodon that binds to a complementary codon on the mRNA.

rRNA (ribosomal RNA) is a type of RNA that is found in ribosomes. Ribosomes are the cellular organelles that are responsible for protein synthesis. rRNA makes up about 60% of the mass of a ribosome, and it plays a role in the assembly of the ribosome and the translation of mRNA into protein

mRNA (messenger RNA) is a type of RNA that carries the genetic information from DNA to the ribosome. It is transcribed from DNA in the nucleus, and then it is transported to the cytoplasm where it is translated into proteins. mRNA is a linear molecule, and it is composed of a sequence of nucleotides that code for a specific protein.

The type of RNA that is used to start the process of replication in a prokaryotic cell is RNA primase. RNA primase is a type of RNA polymerase that synthesizes short RNA primers that are about 10-12 nucleotides long. These primers are used to provide a starting point for DNA polymerase, which then extends the primers to form the new DNA strands.

questions.text: Conjugated histone proteins are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Conjugated histone proteins are a type of conjugated protein that are found in the nucleus of eukaryotic cells. They are composed of a protein core and a non-protein prosthetic group, which is typically a molecule of DNA. Histone proteins are essential for the structure and function of chromosomes. They help to package DNA into a compact form, and they also play a role in gene regulation. The structural role of histone proteins is to form nucleosomes, which are the basic units of chromatin. Nucleosomes are composed of a core of eight histone proteins, around which DNA is wrapped. The DNA is wrapped around the histone proteins in a helical fashion, and this helps to compact the DNA into a much smaller space. The regulatory role of histone proteins is to control the access of transcription factors to DNA. Transcription factors are proteins that bind to DNA and initiate the process of transcription, which is the process of copying DNA into RNA. Histone proteins can either promote or inhibit the binding of transcription factors to DNA, and this can affect the expression of genes.

questions.text: All of the following elements present in all carbohydrates except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Carbon is the cornerstone of organic chemistry, as it forms the basis of organic compounds. Organic molecules are primarily composed of carbon atoms bonded to hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and other elements. These molecules are the building blocks of life and include carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids.DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and RNA (ribonucleic acid) are nucleic acids that store and transmit genetic information. These molecules contain carbon in the form of the sugar-phosphate backbone and the nitrogenous bases adenine, guanine, cytosine, thymine (in DNA), and uracil (in RNA).

Hydrogen is a fundamental element in biology and plays various roles in living organisms. While it is not as prominent as carbon, hydrogen is a critical component of many biological molecules and processes. Hydrogen is a primary component of water, which is essential for all life on Earth. Water is the universal solvent, and it serves as the medium for many biological reactions. Hydrogen bonding between water molecules is also crucial for the structure and function of biological macromolecules.

Oxygen is an essential element in biology and plays a central role in various biological processes, primarily related to energy production and metabolism.Oxygen is crucial for aerobic respiration, a metabolic process used by most living organisms, including animals and many microorganisms. During cellular respiration, cells use oxygen to break down organic molecules (such as glucose) to produce energy in the form of adenosine triphosphate (ATP).Hemoglobin in red blood cells binds to oxygen in the lungs and transports it to tissues throughout the body. Myoglobin, a similar oxygen-binding protein, stores oxygen in muscle cells, providing a reserve for energy production during strenuous physical activity.

Nitrogen is an essential element in biology and plays a central role in various biological processes. Nitrogen is a fundamental component of amino acids, which are the building blocks of proteins. Amino acids contain an amino group (NH2), which contributes nitrogen to the structure. The sequence and arrangement of amino acids in proteins determine their three-dimensional structure and function in biological processes.Nitrogen is a key element in nucleic acids, including DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and RNA (ribonucleic acid). Nitrogenous bases, such as adenine (A), cytosine (C), guanine (G), thymine (T) in DNA, and uracil (U) in RNA, are essential components of these molecules. The sequence of nitrogenous bases encodes genetic information and plays a central role in heredity and genetic expression.Nitrogen is essential for the synthesis of new proteins within cells. During protein synthesis, ribosomes assemble amino acids into polypeptide chains based on the genetic information encoded in DNA and RNA.

They are the most abundant organic compounds in living organisms, and they play a variety of important roles, including energy storage, structure, and signaling. The basic unit of a carbohydrate is a sugar molecule. Sugar molecules can be linked together to form larger molecules, such as starch, cellulose, and glycogen.

The fatty acids can be saturated or unsaturated, and they can be of different lengths. The type of fatty acids that make up an acylglycerol determines its properties.For example, acylglycerols with saturated fatty acids are solid at room temperature, while acylglycerols with unsaturated fatty acids are liquid at room temperature. Acylglycerols with long fatty acids are more viscous than acylglycerols with short fatty acids.

Cuticle is an example of waxes.The cuticle is an example of a protective layer. It is a thin, waxy layer that covers the outside of plants, animals, and some fungi. The cuticle helps to protect the underlying tissues from water loss, infection, and mechanical damage. In plants, the cuticle is made up of cutin, a polymer of fatty acids. Cutin is a very hydrophobic molecule, which means that it repels water. This helps to prevent water loss from the plant's leaves and other exposed tissues. The cuticle also helps to protect the plant from infection by pathogens.

Phospholipids are a type of lipid that is found in all living cells. They are made up of a glycerol molecule, two fatty acids, and a phosphate group. The phosphate group is what gives phospholipids their unique properties.Phospholipids are amphipathic molecules, which means that they have both hydrophilic (water-loving) and hydrophobic (water-fearing) parts. The hydrophilic part is the phosphate group, and the hydrophobic parts are the fatty acids.This amphipathic nature of phospholipids allows them to form bilayers. A bilayer is a two-layer structure in which the hydrophilic parts of the phospholipids face the water, and the hydrophobic parts face each other. Bilayers are the basic structure of cell membranes.

Terpenoids are a large and diverse class of organic compounds that are found in all living things. They are derived from five-carbon isoprene units, which can be linked together in a variety of ways to form different terpenoids. Terpenoids have a wide range of biological activities, including: Antimicrobial: Terpenoids can have antimicrobial properties, meaning that they can kill or inhibit the growth of bacteria, fungi, and other microorganisms. Antioxidant: Terpenoids can have antioxidant properties, meaning that they can help to protect cells from damage caused by free radicals. Anti-inflammatory: Terpenoids can have anti-inflammatory properties, meaning that they can help to reduce inflammation. Immunomodulatory: Terpenoids can have immunomodulatory properties, meaning that they can help to regulate the immune system. Hormonal: Terpenoids can have hormonal properties, meaning that they can mimic or block the effects of hormones.

questions.text: Which of these is constituent of waxes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Long-chain alkanes are not a constituent of waxes. Waxes are esters of fatty acids and long-chain alcohols. They are not made up of only long-chain alkanes. However, long-chain alkanes can be found in some waxes. For example, carnauba wax, which is a hard wax that is obtained from the leaves of the Brazilian palm Copernicia prunifera, contains long-chain alkanes. However, these long-chain alkanes are not the main constituent of carnauba wax. The main constituent of carnauba wax is a mixture of esters of fatty acids and long-chain alcohols.

Alcohols are not a constituent of waxes. Waxes are esters of fatty acids and long-chain alcohols. The alcohol in a wax is typically a long-chain, saturated alcohol with 16 to 36 carbon atoms. For example, beeswax is composed of myricyl palmitate, which is an ester of myristic acid and palmitic acid. Alcohols are not found in waxes because they are too polar. Polar molecules are attracted to water, and waxes are hydrophobic, meaning that they are not attracted to water. If alcohols were present in waxes, they would make the waxes more polar and less hydrophobic. This would make the waxes less water-resistant and less effective at protecting surfaces.

Aldehydes are not a constituent of waxes. Waxes are esters of long-chain fatty acids and long-chain alcohols. They are typically hydrophobic and have a high melting point. Aldehydes, on the other hand, are typically hydrophilic and have a low melting point. As a result, they are not compatible with the structure and properties of waxes.

options.explanation: Yes, ATP is called the "energy currency of the cell" because it is used to store and release energy for cellular processes. ATP is a molecule that is made up of three phosphate groups attached to a ribose sugar. The bonds between the phosphate groups are high-energy bonds, which means that they can be broken to release energy.

Yes, ATP does contain ribose sugar. Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is a molecule that is found in all living cells. It is the body's main source of energy, and it is used to power many different cellular processes. ATP is made up of three components: Adenine: This is a nitrogenous base. Ribose: This is a pentose sugar. Three phosphate groups: These are attached to the ribose sugar.

No, ATP does not contain three high-energy bonds. It actually contains two high-energy bonds. The high-energy bonds in ATP are the bonds between the phosphate groups. The third phosphate group in ATP is not considered to be a high-energy bond because it is not as easily broken. This is because the third phosphate group is linked to the second phosphate group by a phosphoanhydride bond, which is a very strong bond. The two high-energy bonds in ATP are located between the first and second phosphate groups, and between the second and third phosphate groups. When these bonds are broken, they release about 7.3 kcal/mol of energy each. This energy can be used to power a variety of cellular processes, such as muscle contraction, protein synthesis, and active transport.

questions.text: Which one of the following is an example of reducing sugar?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, sucrose is not a reducing sugar. Reducing sugars are sugars that have a free aldehyde or ketone group. This means that they can reduce other molecules, such as the copper ion in Benedict's reagent. Sucrose does not have a free aldehyde or ketone group, so it cannot reduce other molecules. Sucrose is a disaccharide, which means that it is made up of two monosaccharides, glucose and fructose. The glucose and fructose molecules in sucrose are linked together by a glycosidic bond, which is a type of chemical bond that does not allow the free aldehyde or ketone group to be exposed.

No, starch is not a reducing sugar. Reducing sugars are sugars that have a free aldehyde or ketone group. Starch does not have a free aldehyde or ketone group, so it is not a reducing sugar. Reducing sugars can be oxidized, which means that they can lose electrons. This oxidation can be detected by a chemical test called the Benedict's test. Starch does not give a positive result for the Benedict's test, so it is not a reducing sugar.

That's right. Maltose is a reducing sugar. Reducing sugars are carbohydrates that have an aldehyde or ketone group that can be oxidized. This means that they can act as reducing agents, which can donate electrons to other molecules. Maltose is a disaccharide, which means that it is made up of two sugar molecules. The two sugar molecules in maltose are glucose molecules. Maltose is formed when two glucose molecules are joined together by a 1-4 glycosidic bond.

No, cellulose is not an example of a reducing sugar. Reducing sugars are sugars that have a free aldehyde or ketone group. Cellulose does not have a free aldehyde or ketone group, so it is not a reducing sugar. Reducing sugars can be oxidized by other molecules, and they can also reduce other molecules. This is because the free aldehyde or ketone group can be oxidized to form an acid. The ability of reducing sugars to reduce other molecules is used in a variety of analytical tests, such as the Benedict's test and the Fehling's test

options.explanation: Maltose is a disaccharide, which means it is composed of two simple sugar molecules or monosaccharides. Specifically, maltose is made up of two glucose molecules linked together. Its chemical formula is C12H22O11, and it is sometimes referred to as "malt sugar."Maltose is commonly found in various food sources, including grains, where it is produced during the enzymatic breakdown of starch.

Lactose is a disaccharide sugar, which means it is composed of two simple sugar molecules or monosaccharides. In the case of lactose, it is made up of one molecule of glucose and one molecule of galactose linked together. Its chemical formula is C12H22O11.Lactose is commonly found in milk and dairy products, making it sometimes referred to as "milk sugar." It is the primary carbohydrate found in the milk of mammals, including cows, goats, and humans. Lactose serves as a source of energy for newborns and young mammals.

Raffinose is a complex carbohydrate or oligosaccharide composed of three simple sugar molecules: galactose, glucose, and fructose. Its chemical formula is C18H32O16. Raffinose is commonly found in various plants and is a type of sugar that is not as easily digested by humans as simpler sugars like glucose or sucrose (table sugar).

Sugar cane and sugar beet are two major commercial sources of sucrose production.

questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Carbohydrates are one of the four major classes of organic compounds in living organisms, along with proteins, lipids (fats), and nucleic acids. Carbohydrates are composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms and serve as a primary source of energy for both plants and animals. They play essential roles in various biological processes.

Nucleic acids are one of the two major classes of macromolecules (the other being proteins) and are essential to the storage and transmission of genetic information in living organisms. They come in two primary forms: deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and ribonucleic acid (RNA).

Proteins are large, complex molecules that play a fundamental role in the structure and function of living organisms. They are one of the essential macromolecules, along with carbohydrates, lipids, and nucleic acids, and they are involved in a wide range of biological processes.Proteins are made up of long chains of amino acids. Each amino acid consists of a central carbon atom (the alpha carbon) bonded to an amino group, a carboxyl group, a hydrogen atom, and a variable side chain (R group). The sequence of amino acids in a protein determines its unique structure and function.

Lipids are a diverse group of organic molecules that are important for various biological functions in living organisms. They are hydrophobic (insoluble in water) and include a wide range of molecules, some of which are essential for life. Lipids encompass a variety of molecules, including triglycerides (fats and oils), phospholipids, steroids, and waxes. Each type of lipid has specific roles in living organisms.Triglycerides are the primary form of stored energy in the body. They are found in adipose tissue and serve as a long-term energy reserve. When broken down, they release energy for metabolic processes.

options.explanation: A monosaccharide is the simplest form of a carbohydrate, often referred to as a simple sugar. These molecules are the basic building blocks of more complex carbohydrates like disaccharides, oligosaccharides, and polysaccharides. Monosaccharides have the general molecular formula (CH2O)n, where "n" can vary from three to seven or more. The most common monosaccharides are trioses (three carbon atoms), tetroses (four carbon atoms), pentoses (five carbon atoms), and hexoses (six carbon atoms). Oligosaccharides are carbohydrates composed of a relatively small number of monosaccharide units, typically ranging from 3 to 10 sugar molecules. These sugar molecules are linked together by glycosidic bonds, similar to how monosaccharides are joined to form disaccharides. Oligosaccharides are less complex than polysaccharides, which can consist of hundreds or thousands of monosaccharide units.

options.explanation: Plant cell walls are rigid structures that surround plant cells, providing support and protection for the cell. They are composed of various complex molecules, primarily cellulose, hemicellulose, and pectin.Cellulose is the most abundant component of plant cell walls and consists of long chains of glucose molecules linked together. These chains form microfibrils that provide tensile strength to the cell wall. Cellulose is a key structural component in the cell wall and plays a crucial role in maintaining cell shape and preventing cell bursting.

Algal cells are the cells of algae, a diverse group of photosynthetic organisms that can be found in various aquatic environments, ranging from freshwater to marine ecosystems. Algae can be unicellular, multicellular, or colonial, and they come in a wide array of shapes and sizes. While there is a great diversity of algae, they share some common characteristics in terms of their cell structure and function.

Fungal cells are the building blocks of fungi, which comprise a diverse group of eukaryotic microorganisms, including yeasts, molds, and mushrooms. Fungal cells share some common characteristics with other eukaryotic cells, but they also have some unique features. Fungal cells typically have a rigid cell wall made of complex carbohydrates, primarily chitin. Chitin is a strong and flexible material that provides structural support to the cell and protection against environmental stress. The composition and thickness of the cell wall can vary among different fungal species.

A biological membrane, also known as a cell membrane or plasma membrane, is a crucial structural component found in all living cells. It separates the interior of the cell from its external environment and plays a fundamental role in maintaining cell integrity and controlling the flow of materials into and out of the cell. Biological membranes are composed of a double layer of lipids, primarily phospholipids, with proteins interspersed within this lipid bilayer. The lipid bilayer is often referred to as the lipid membrane. The proteins embedded in the membrane can vary in size and function.

questions.explanation: Basic framework of cellular membranes is provided by proteins and lipids, however carbohydrates are conjugated with them to synthesize glycoproteins and glycolipids which act as cell markers and tissue markers.

options.explanation: Carbohydrates are one of the four major classes of organic compounds in living organisms, along with proteins, lipids (fats), and nucleic acids. Carbohydrates are composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms and serve as a primary source of energy for both plants and animals. They play essential roles in various biological processes.

Monosaccharides are the simplest and most basic form of carbohydrates. They are also known as simple sugars and are composed of single sugar molecules. Monosaccharides are the fundamental building blocks of more complex carbohydrates and serve as a primary source of energy for living organisms. These sugars have the general molecular formula (CH2O)n, where "n" can vary, and they come in various forms.

Oligosaccharides are carbohydrates composed of a relatively small number of monosaccharide units, typically ranging from 3 to 10 sugar molecules. These sugar molecules are linked together by glycosidic bonds, similar to how monosaccharides are joined to form disaccharides. Oligosaccharides are less complex than polysaccharides, which can consist of hundreds or thousands of monosaccharide units.

Polysaccharides are large, complex carbohydrates composed of multiple monosaccharide units linked together by glycosidic bonds. These macromolecules serve various structural and storage functions in living organisms. Polysaccharides are essential components of biological systems, and they come in different forms with distinct properties.

options.explanation: In the context of biology, "DAP" typically stands for "Diaminopimelic acid." Diaminopimelic acid is an amino acid found in the cell walls of certain bacteria. It is an essential component of the peptidoglycan layer, a structural feature of bacterial cell walls. Peptidoglycan is a complex polymer made up of long chains of sugars (glycan chains) cross-linked by short peptides. Diaminopimelic acid is one of the amino acids found in the peptide chains of peptidoglycan. The presence of diaminopimelic acid in peptidoglycan is a characteristic feature that distinguishes bacterial cell walls from those of other organisms.

RuBP stands for "Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate," which is a critical molecule in the process of photosynthesis. Photosynthesis is the biological process by which green plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy in the form of glucose (sugar). Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate plays a central role in the initial steps of the Calvin cycle, which is one of the key stages of photosynthesis.

G6P stands for "Glucose-6-Phosphate," which is an important molecule in glucose metabolism and energy production in living cells. It is a phosphorylated form of glucose, meaning it has a phosphate group attached to it. G6P plays a central role in various metabolic pathways, including glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, and the pentose phosphate pathway.

G3P stands for "Glyceraldehyde-3-Phosphate," which is a crucial molecule in the process of glycolysis and various metabolic pathways. Glycolysis is a central metabolic pathway that occurs in the cytoplasm of cells, and G3P is one of its key intermediates.

options.explanation: Glyceraldehyde is a simple sugar, specifically a monosaccharide, with the molecular formula C3H6O3. It is an important molecule in biochemistry and is a key component in various metabolic pathways. There are two stereoisomers of glyceraldehyde: D-glyceraldehyde and L-glyceraldehyde, which are mirror images of each other and are enantiomers.

Ribose is a five-carbon sugar (pentose) with the molecular formula C5H10O5. It is a monosaccharide, which means it is a simple sugar and cannot be further hydrolyzed to simpler sugars. Ribose plays a crucial role in biochemistry and is an essential component of nucleic acids, including ribonucleic acid (RNA).

Fructose is a monosaccharide, a simple sugar, with the molecular formula C6H12O6. It is one of the three most common dietary monosaccharides, along with glucose and galactose. Fructose is naturally found in many fruits, vegetables, and honey and is often associated with its sweet taste.

Glucose is a simple sugar, or monosaccharide, with the chemical formula C6H12O6. It is a key source of energy for living organisms, and its metabolism plays a central role in various biological processes. Glucose is a primary source of energy for cells in many organisms, including humans. It serves as fuel for cellular respiration, a process in which glucose is broken down to produce adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the molecule that stores and transfers energy within cells.

questions.text: _ and _ are most common monosaccharides.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Trioses and tetroses are types of monosaccharides, which are the simplest form of carbohydrates. Monosaccharides are organic compounds consisting of a single sugar molecule. Glyceraldehyde, for example, is an intermediate in glycolysis and serves as a source of energy for cells.. While tetrose sugars are less common than trioses, they are still involved in metabolic processes and can serve as precursors for larger carbohydrate molecules.

Tetroses and pentoses are two distinct classes of monosaccharides, which are simple sugars composed of a single sugar molecule. Tetroses are monosaccharides that contain four carbon atoms in their molecular structure. Examples of tetroses include erythrose and threose. Tetroses are relatively rare in nature compared to other monosaccharides like pentoses and hexoses. Pentoses are monosaccharides that contain five carbon atoms in their molecular structure. Examples of pentoses include ribose and deoxyribose. Pentoses are more common and have important roles in biological processes. For example, ribose is a component of ribonucleic acid (RNA), while deoxyribose is a component of deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA).

Pentoses and hexoses are two distinct classes of monosaccharides, which are simple sugars composed of a single sugar molecule.Pentoses are monosaccharides that contain five carbon atoms in their molecular structure. Common examples of pentoses include ribose and deoxyribose. Pentoses are important in various biological processes. Ribose is a key component of ribonucleic acid (RNA), while deoxyribose is a component of deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA). These sugars are essential for genetic information storage and transfer. Hexoses are monosaccharides that contain six carbon atoms in their molecular structure. The most common and well-known hexose is glucose, but other hexoses include fructose and galactose. Hexoses are central to energy metabolism and are used as a primary source of energy in living organisms. Glucose, for example, is a critical sugar found in the bloodstream and is a primary energy source for cells.

Hexoses and heptoses are two different classes of monosaccharides, which are simple sugars composed of a single sugar molecule.Hexoses are monosaccharides that contain six carbon atoms in their molecular structure. Common examples of hexoses include glucose, fructose, and galactose. Hexoses are central to energy metabolism and serve as primary sources of energy for living organisms. For example, glucose is a key sugar found in the bloodstream and is the primary energy source for cells. Heptoses are monosaccharides that contain seven carbon atoms in their molecular structure. While hexoses are more common and well-known, heptoses are relatively rare in nature. Heptoses can be found in certain complex carbohydrates, lipopolysaccharides, and other biomolecules.

questions.explanation: Pentoses being part of nucleic acids and hexoses (glucose) being substrate for aerobic respiration are most common carbohydrates of nature.

options.explanation: Glucopyranose, often simply referred to as glucose, is a monosaccharide and one of the most important and abundant sugars in biology. It is a hexose sugar, meaning it contains six carbon atoms in its molecular structure. Glucose plays a central role in energy metabolism and is a primary source of energy for living organisms.

Fructofuranose is a term used to describe a form of fructose, which is a monosaccharide, or simple sugar. More specifically, "fructofuranose" refers to a specific structural isomer of fructose in which the sugar molecule has a furanose ring structure. In the furanose form of fructose, the sugar ring consists of a five-membered ring containing four carbon atoms and one oxygen atom. This is in contrast to the linear form of fructose, which is an open-chain structure.

Ribofuranose is a term used to describe the furanose form of ribose, which is a type of monosaccharide. Ribose is a five-carbon sugar, and when it takes on a furanose ring structure, it forms a ring consisting of a five-membered ring with four carbon atoms and one oxygen atom. This is in contrast to the linear or open-chain structure of ribose.

Deoxyribofuranose is a term used to describe the furanose form of deoxyribose, which is a modified sugar molecule. Deoxyribose is a five-carbon sugar, and when it takes on a furanose ring structure, it forms a ring consisting of a five-membered ring with four carbon atoms and one oxygen atom. This furanose ring structure is a key component of deoxyribose, which is found in deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA).

questions.text: The ones yielding two monosaccharides on hydrolysis are:

options.explanation: Trisaccharides: Trisaccharides are carbohydrates composed of three monosaccharide units linked together. Hydrolysis of a trisaccharide breaks the chemical bonds between the monosaccharide units, resulting in the formation of two monosaccharides. An example of a trisaccharide is raffinose.

Tetrasaccharides: Tetrasaccharides are carbohydrates composed of four monosaccharide units linked together. Hydrolysis of a tetrasaccharide breaks the chemical bonds between the monosaccharide units, resulting in the formation of two monosaccharides. An example of a tetrasaccharide is stachyose.

Pentasaccharides: Pentasaccharides are carbohydrates composed of five monosaccharide units linked together. Hydrolysis of a pentasaccharide breaks the chemical bonds between the monosaccharide units, resulting in the formation of two monosaccharides. An example of a pentasaccharide is verbascose.

Disaccharides: Disaccharides are carbohydrates composed of two monosaccharide units linked together. Hydrolysis of a disaccharide breaks the chemical bond between the monosaccharide units, resulting in the formation of two monosaccharides. Common examples of disaccharides include sucrose, lactose, and maltose.

questions.text: The most complex and the most abundant carbohydrates in nature are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Monosaccharides: Monosaccharides are the simplest form of carbohydrates. They consist of a single sugar unit and cannot be further hydrolyzed into smaller carbohydrates. Examples of monosaccharides include glucose, fructose, and galactose. While monosaccharides are important building blocks for more complex carbohydrates, they are not considered the most complex and abundant carbohydrates in nature.

Oligosaccharides are carbohydrates composed of a small number of monosaccharide units (typically 3-10 units) linked together. They are intermediate in complexity between monosaccharides and polysaccharides. Oligosaccharides play important roles in various biological processes and can be found in foods like beans, lentils, and certain fruits. While they are more complex than monosaccharides, they are not considered the most complex and abundant carbohydrates in nature.

Disaccharides are carbohydrates composed of two monosaccharide units linked together. Examples of disaccharides include sucrose, lactose, and maltose. Disaccharides are more complex than monosaccharides and play a significant role as energy sources in organisms. However, they are not considered the most complex and abundant carbohydrates in nature.

Polysaccharides: Polysaccharides are the most complex and abundant carbohydrates in nature. They consist of long chains of monosaccharide units linked together. Examples of polysaccharides include starch, cellulose, and glycogen. Polysaccharides have various functions, including energy storage, structural support, and cell signaling. They can be found in plants, animals, and microorganisms.

questions.explanation: Polysaccharides: Polysaccharides are the most complex and abundant carbohydrates in nature. They consist of long chains of monosaccharide units linked together. Examples of polysaccharides include starch, cellulose, and glycogen. Polysaccharides have various functions, including energy storage, structural support, and cell signaling. They can be found in plants, animals, and microorganisms.

questions.text: The carbohydrates which are only sparingly soluble in water are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Monosaccharides are the simplest form of carbohydrates, consisting of a single sugar unit. Most monosaccharides, such as glucose and fructose, are readily soluble in water due to their small molecular size and hydrophilic nature. They can form aqueous solutions without difficulty. Therefore, monosaccharides are not sparingly soluble in water.

Disaccharides are carbohydrates composed of two monosaccharide units linked together. Examples include sucrose, lactose, and maltose. Disaccharides are relatively soluble in water, although their solubility may vary depending on temperature and concentration. These compounds can dissolve in water to form sweet-tasting solutions.

Oligosaccharides are carbohydrates composed of a few (typically 3-10) monosaccharide units linked together. Their solubility in water can vary depending on the specific structure and functional groups present. While some oligosaccharides may exhibit limited solubility, many are still moderately soluble in water.

Polysaccharides are complex carbohydrates composed of long chains of monosaccharide units. Due to their large molecular size and extensive branching or cross-linking, most polysaccharides have limited solubility in water. Examples include cellulose, chitin, and certain types of starch. These compounds often form colloidal suspensions or insoluble precipitates in water.

questions.explanation: Correct answer: D) Polysaccharides are the carbohydrates that are only sparingly soluble in water.

questions.text: It consists of an unbranched chain of α glucose monomers:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Amylopectin is a branched polysaccharide made up of α-glucose monomers. Unlike amylose, which is unbranched, amylopectin has a highly branched structure. It consists of both linear and branched segments of α-glucose units. Each branch in amylopectin is connected by an α-1,6-glycosidic bond.

Glycogen is a highly branched polysaccharide composed of α-glucose monomers. It serves as the main storage form of glucose in animals, including humans. Similar to amylopectin, glycogen has a highly branched structure with both linear and branched segments. The branches are connected by α-1,6-glycosidic bonds.

Glycogen is a highly branched polysaccharide composed of α-glucose monomers. It serves as the main storage form of glucose in animals, including humans. Similar to amylopectin, glycogen has a highly branched structure with both linear and branched segments. The branches are connected by α-1,6-glycosidic bonds.

Amylose is an unbranched polysaccharide composed of α-glucose monomers. It forms a linear chain of α-glucose units that are connected by α-1,4-glycosidic bonds. Amylose is a component of starch, which is the storage form of glucose in plants.

questions.explanation: Correct option: D) Amylose consists of an unbranched chain of α-glucose monomers.

This option is not correct. Amylopectin starches are branched, not unbranched. Insolubility is generally associated with certain forms of cellulose, not amylopectin.

This option is the correct answer. Amylopectin starches have a branched structure, with many branches connected to the main chain of α-glucose units by α-1,6-glycosidic bonds. Due to their complex structure, amylopectin starches are generally insoluble in both hot and cold water. They require specific conditions, such as heating or enzymatic treatment, to break down and become soluble.

questions.text: _ gives blue color with iodine.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glycogen is a highly branched polysaccharide that serves as the main storage form of glucose in animals. It consists of α-glucose units linked by α-1,4-glycosidic bonds with occasional α-1,6-glycosidic branch points. Glycogen does not give a blue color with iodine. Instead, it may show a reddish-brown color.

Cellulose is a linear polysaccharide composed of β-glucose units linked by β-1,4-glycosidic bonds. It forms the structural component of the cell walls in plants. Cellulose does not give a blue color with iodine. It generally remains colorless or may show a pale yellowish color.

Dextrin is a group of small, partially hydrolyzed starch molecules. It is derived from starch by the action of heat, enzymes, or acids. Depending on the degree of hydrolysis, dextrin can have varying structures. Dextrin may give a yellowish or reddish-brown color with iodine, but it does not typically give a blue color.

Starches consist of two main components: amylose and amylopectin. Both amylose and amylopectin, which are polysaccharides composed of α-glucose units, give a blue color with iodine. The blue color arises due to the formation of a complex between iodine molecules and the helical structure of amylose or the branched structure of amylopectin.

questions.explanation: The iodine test is used to test the presence of starch. Starch turns its color to dark blue-black upon addition of aqueous solutions of the triodide anion, due to the formation of an intermolecular charge- transfer complex.

questions.text: It is found abundantly in muscle and liver cells:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Starch is a carbohydrate that serves as a storage form of glucose in plants. It is found abundantly in plant cells, particularly in storage organs like roots, tubers, and seeds. However, it is not found abundantly in muscle and liver cells.

Chitin is a polysaccharide that forms the structural component of the exoskeletons of arthropods (such as insects and crustaceans) and the cell walls of fungi. While it is abundant in certain organisms, it is not primarily found in muscle and liver cells.

Glycogen is a polysaccharide that serves as the main storage form of glucose in animals, including humans. It is abundantly found in muscle and liver cells, where it is stored to provide a readily available source of energy.

Amylose is a component of starch, a storage polysaccharide in plants. While amylose is present in starch, it is not primarily found in muscle and liver cells. It is worth noting that glycogen, which was mentioned in option C, is structurally similar to amylose.

questions.explanation: In animals, surplus glucose is converted into insoluble glycogen and then it is stored in muscle and liver cells. This conversion is regulated by insulin.

questions.text: It is highly insoluble in water:

options.explanation: Glycogen is a polysaccharide that serves as the primary storage form of glucose in animals, including humans. While it is soluble in water, it is not highly insoluble. Glycogen can be readily hydrolyzed and broken down into glucose molecules when needed for energy.

Amylose is a linear chain polysaccharide and a component of starch, a storage polysaccharide in plants. It is moderately soluble in water, forming colloidal suspensions. However, it is not highly insoluble.

Cellulose is a polysaccharide and the main structural component of plant cell walls. It is highly insoluble in water due to its extensive hydrogen bonding between cellulose chains. Cellulose provides rigidity and strength to plant tissues.

Agar is a complex mixture of polysaccharides extracted from certain types of seaweed. While it is not highly insoluble, it forms a gel-like substance in water due to its unique properties. Agar is commonly used in laboratories and cooking for its gelling abilities.

questions.explanation: Correct option: C) Cellulose is highly insoluble in water.

questions.text: The bacteria, yeast and protozoans found in the digestive tract of herbivores secrete:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Amylase is an enzyme that helps break down starch into simpler sugars such as maltose and glucose. It is primarily produced in the salivary glands and pancreas of animals, including humans. While herbivores may produce amylase, it is not primarily secreted by the bacteria, yeast, or protozoans found in their digestive tracts.

Cellulase is an enzyme that breaks down cellulose, a complex carbohydrate found in plant cell walls. Bacteria, yeast, and certain protozoans present in the digestive tracts of herbivores secrete cellulase to help digest cellulose. These microorganisms produce cellulase because herbivores themselves lack the necessary enzymes to break down cellulose.

Lipase is an enzyme that aids in the digestion of fats or lipids. It breaks down lipids into fatty acids and glycerol. While lipase is important for lipid digestion, it is not primarily secreted by the bacteria, yeast, or protozoans found in the digestive tracts of herbivores.

Ptyalin is a type of amylase that is found in saliva. It helps initiate the breakdown of starches into simpler sugars in the mouth during the process of digestion. However, it is not primarily secreted by the bacteria, yeast, or protozoans found in the digestive tracts of herbivores.

questions.explanation: Cellulase is any of several enzymes produced chiefly by fungi, bacteria and protozoans that catalyze the cellulolysis, the decomposition of cellulose and of some related polysaccharides.

questions.text: It gives no color with iodine:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Amylose is a linear polysaccharide and a component of starch, a storage carbohydrate in plants. When iodine is added to amylose, it forms a deep blue or violet color complex due to the helical structure of amylose. Therefore, amylose does give a color (blue or violet) with iodine.

Amylopectin is a branched polysaccharide and another component of starch. Similar to amylose, amylopectin also gives a blue or violet color with iodine due to its helical structure. Therefore, amylopectin does give a color (blue or violet) with iodine.

Glycogen is a highly branched polysaccharide and serves as the main storage form of glucose in animals, including humans. Like amylose and amylopectin, glycogen also gives a blue or violet color with iodine due to its structural similarity to amylose. Therefore, glycogen does give a color (blue or violet) with iodine.

Cellulose is a linear polysaccharide and the main structural component of plant cell walls. Unlike amylose, amylopectin, and glycogen, cellulose does not give a color with iodine. The lack of color reaction is because cellulose has a different structure and does not form the same complex with iodine.

questions.explanation: Correct option: D) Cellulose gives no color with iodine.

questions.text: They are insoluble in water and soluble in organic solvents:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nucleic acids, such as DNA and RNA, are large biomolecules that store and transmit genetic information. They are typically soluble in water due to their polar nature and the presence of charged phosphate groups. However, nucleic acids are not soluble in organic solvents. They dissolve and interact primarily in aqueous environments.

Carbohydrates, including sugars and starches, are generally soluble in water. However, some complex carbohydrates like cellulose and chitin may have limited solubility. Carbohydrates are not typically soluble in organic solvents.

Lipids are a diverse group of biomolecules that include fats, oils, waxes, and phospholipids. Lipids are insoluble in water due to their nonpolar nature. However, they are soluble in organic solvents such as ethanol, chloroform, and benzene. This solubility behavior is due to the hydrophobic nature of lipids.

Proteins are large biomolecules composed of amino acids that perform a wide range of functions in living organisms. Proteins are generally soluble in water due to the presence of polar and charged amino acid residues. However, they are not typically soluble in organic solvents. Proteins fold into complex three-dimensional structures that are stabilized by hydrophilic interactions with water molecules.

questions.explanation: Correct option: C) Lipids are insoluble in water and soluble in organic solvents.

questions.text: Lipids store double amount of energy, as compared to the equal amount of carbohydrates, because of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lipids store double the amount of energy compared to an equal amount of carbohydrates primarily because they have a higher proportion of C-H bonds. Lipids are hydrocarbons, meaning they consist of long hydrocarbon chains that contain many C-H bonds. These carbon-hydrogen bonds contain a high amount of energy, which is released during metabolism. The presence of more C-H bonds in lipids allows for a greater energy storage capacity.

While lipids can contain small amounts of nitrogen in certain types such as phospholipids, the primary reason for the higher energy storage capacity of lipids compared to carbohydrates is not due to a higher proportion of C-N bonds.

Carbohydrates, not lipids, have a higher proportion of C-O bonds. Carbohydrates consist of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms, and their structure involves multiple C-O bonds. Lipids, on the other hand, have a higher proportion of C-H bonds.

C-O-P bonds are primarily associated with compounds such as phosphates, which are not major components of lipids. Lipids, particularly triglycerides, do not contain a significant proportion of C-O-P bonds.

questions.explanation: Correct option: A) Lipids store double the amount of energy compared to an equal amount of carbohydrates because of their higher proportion of C-H bonds.

questions.text: Pick up the one which is not a lipid:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acylglycerols, also known as triglycerides, and waxes are types of lipids. They both consist of fatty acid chains attached to a glycerol backbone. Acylglycerols are the main components of dietary fats and oils, while waxes are found in various natural sources, including plant cuticles and animal coatings.

Phospholipids and sphingolipids are also types of lipids. Phospholipids are a major component of cell membranes and consist of a hydrophilic head and two hydrophobic fatty acid tails. Sphingolipids are another class of lipids found in cell membranes and have a sphingosine backbone.

Carotenoids and steroids are not lipids. Carotenoids are a group of pigments found in plants, algae, and some bacteria. They are responsible for the vibrant colors in fruits and vegetables. Steroids, such as cholesterol and hormones like testosterone and estrogen, are organic compounds with a specific four-ring structure.

These are not lipids. Dextrins and pectins are types of carbohydrates. Dextrins are produced by the partial hydrolysis of starch and are commonly used as food additives. Pectins are complex polysaccharides found in the cell walls of plants and are involved in maintaining cell structure.

questions.explanation: Correct option: D) Dextrins and pectins are not lipids.

questions.text: Chemically _ can be defined as esters of fatty acids and alcohol.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phospholipids are a class of lipids that have a phosphate group in their structure. They consist of a glycerol backbone, two fatty acid chains, and a phosphate group. Phospholipids are major components of cell membranes. While they are esters of fatty acids and alcohol (glycerol), their structure includes a phosphate group, making them different from the given definition.

Waxes are also lipids, but they are esters of long-chain fatty acids with long-chain alcohols. They have diverse functions, including providing waterproofing and protection for various organisms and objects.

Acylglycerols, also known as glycerides or triglycerides, are esters of fatty acids and glycerol. They are the main constituents of dietary fats and oils. Acylglycerols consist of three fatty acid chains esterified to a glycerol molecule.

Terpenoids, also known as isoprenoids, are a diverse group of naturally occurring compounds derived from the five-carbon molecule isoprene. While terpenoids are an important class of compounds with various biological functions, they do not fit the given definition of esters of fatty acids and alcohol.

questions.explanation: Correct option: C) Acylglycerols can be defined as esters of fatty acids and alcohol.

questions.text: A compound produced as a result of a chemical reaction of an alcohol with an acid, where a water molecule is released, is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acylglycerols, also known as glycerides or triglycerides, are a type of lipid that consists of glycerol esterified with fatty acids. While they are esters formed from glycerol and fatty acids, they are not specifically related to the given definition in the question. Acylglycerols serve as a major storage form of fats in the body.

An ester is a compound produced from the reaction between an alcohol and an acid, resulting in the release of a water molecule. Esters have a general structure of RCOOR', where R and R' represent organic groups. They are commonly used in various applications, such as flavorings, fragrances, and solvents.

Fatty acids are carboxylic acids with long hydrocarbon chains. While they are involved in the formation of esters, fatty acids themselves do not match the given definition. Fatty acids are building blocks for various lipids, including triglycerides, phospholipids, and waxes.

Waxes are esters formed by the reaction of long-chain fatty acids with long-chain alcohols. While waxes are indeed formed through an esterification reaction, they are not the specific compound described in the question. Waxes serve as protective coatings in many organisms and objects.

questions.explanation: Correct option: B) The compound produced as a result of a chemical reaction of an alcohol with an acid, where a water molecule is released, is called an ester.

questions.text: For ester formation _ is released from alcohol and _ is released from acid, which combine to form a water molecule.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During ester formation, an H (hydrogen atom) is released from the alcohol, and an OH (hydroxyl group) is released from the acid. The released H and OH combine to form a water molecule (H2O). This reaction is known as a condensation reaction or dehydration synthesis.
During ester formation, both the alcohol and the acid do not release hydrogen atoms (H) only. The reaction requires the release of an OH group from the acid to form a water molecule.

During ester formation, the alcohol does not release an OH group. Instead, it releases an H atom. The acid, on the other hand, releases an OH group, which combines with the released H atom from the alcohol to form a water molecule.

Both the alcohol and the acid do not release OH groups only during ester formation. The reaction requires the release of an H atom from the alcohol and an OH group from the acid to form a water molecule.

questions.explanation: Correct option: A) During ester formation, an H (hydrogen atom) is released from the alcohol, and an OH (hydroxyl group) is released from the acid, which combine to form a water molecule.

questions.text: Fatty acids contain even number of carbon atoms in straight chain attached with _ atoms and having _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fatty acids contain an even number of carbon atoms in a straight chain attached with hydrogen atoms and have an acidic group, which is a carboxyl group (-COOH) at one end. The carboxyl group is responsible for the acidic properties of fatty acids.

Fatty acids do not have an alcohol group (-OH) attached to the carbon chain. While alcohols are functional groups commonly found in various molecules, fatty acids specifically have a carboxyl group at one end.

While fatty acids do have an oxygen atom in the carboxyl group, it is not referred to as an "acid group." The carboxyl group itself is an acidic functional group due to its ability to release hydrogen ions (H+) and donate a proton.

Fatty acids do not have an alcohol group (-OH) attached to the carbon chain. The hydrogens are attached to the carbon atoms in the chain, and the carboxyl group is responsible for the acidic properties of fatty acids.

questions.explanation: Correct option: A) Fatty acids contain an even number of carbon atoms in a straight chain attached with hydrogen atoms and have an acidic group.

questions.text: The number of carbon atoms in fatty acids may be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option suggests that fatty acids can have a range of 2 to 7 carbon atoms. However, this range is quite limited, and most naturally occurring fatty acids have more than 7 carbon atoms. Fatty acids with fewer carbon atoms are typically found in certain microbial sources or synthetic compounds.

This option suggests that fatty acids can have a range of 3 to 7 carbon atoms. While it is possible to find fatty acids with this range, it is not the most common distribution in nature. Most fatty acids found in biological systems have more than 7 carbon atoms.

This option suggests that fatty acids can have a range of 2 to 30 carbon atoms. This range is more accurate and reflective of the variety of fatty acids found in biological systems. Fatty acids with carbon atom numbers ranging from 2 to 30 can be found in different organisms and sources.

This option suggests that fatty acids can have a range of 2 to 40 carbon atoms. While it is theoretically possible to have fatty acids with up to 40 carbon atoms, such long-chain fatty acids are less common in biological systems. Most naturally occurring fatty acids have a smaller number of carbon atoms.

questions.explanation: Correct option: C) The number of carbon atoms in fatty acids may range from 2 to 30.

questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In animals, fatty acids are typically unbranched, meaning they have a straight chain of carbon atoms. In plants, however, fatty acids can be unbranched, branched, or even contain ring structures. This variability in fatty acid structure contributes to the diversity of lipids found in plants.
While fatty acids in plants can have different structures including branched and ringed forms, the arrangement in animals is typically unbranched.
While fatty acids in plants can have different structures including ringed forms, the arrangement in animals is typically unbranched.
While fatty acids in plants can have different structures including ringed forms, the arrangement in animals is typically unbranched.

questions.text: Solubility of fatty acids in organic solvents and their melting points increase with increase in the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Number of carbon atoms in chain
options.explanation: The solubility of fatty acids in organic solvents and their melting points generally increase with an increase in the number of carbon atoms in the fatty acid chain. Longer fatty acid chains have larger nonpolar regions, which enhance their solubility in nonpolar solvents. Additionally, longer chains also increase the strength of Van der Waals forces between fatty acid molecules, resulting in higher melting points.

options.text: Number of oxygen atoms in chain
options.explanation: The number of oxygen atoms in the fatty acid chain does not directly impact the solubility in organic solvents or the melting points of fatty acids. While oxygen atoms may be present in functional groups like carboxyl (-COOH) or hydroxyl (-OH), their influence on solubility and melting points is secondary to the carbon chain length.

options.text: Number of hydrogen atoms in chain
options.explanation: The number of hydrogen atoms in the fatty acid chain does not have a significant influence on the solubility in organic solvents or the melting points of fatty acids. While hydrogen atoms are present in the fatty acid structure, they do not directly affect the solubility or melting points as much as the carbon chain length.

options.text: Number of acid groups in chain
options.explanation: The number of acid groups (-COOH) in the fatty acid chain does not directly affect the solubility in organic solvents or the melting points of fatty acids. The presence of acid groups primarily contributes to the acidic properties of fatty acids and their ability to form salts with bases.

questions.explanation: Correct option: A) The solubility of fatty acids in organic solvents and their melting points increase with an increase in the number of carbon atoms in the chain.

questions.text: _ is much more soluble in organic solvent than _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option suggests that acetic acid is much more soluble in an organic solvent than butyric acid. Acetic acid is a short-chain fatty acid with two carbon atoms, while butyric acid is a short-chain fatty acid with four carbon atoms. Generally, as the chain length of a fatty acid increases, its solubility in organic solvents decreases.

This option suggests that butyric acid is much more soluble in an organic solvent than palmitic acid. Butyric acid is a short-chain fatty acid with four carbon atoms, while palmitic acid is a long-chain fatty acid with 16 carbon atoms. Short-chain fatty acids tend to be more soluble in organic solvents compared to long-chain fatty acids.

Palmitic acid is much more soluble in organic solvents than butyric acid.This is because:1. Palmitic acid is a long-chain fatty acid (16 carbon atoms), making it non-polar and hydrophobic (water-repelling). It is more compatible with non-polar organic solvents.2. Butyric acid, on the other hand, is a short-chain fatty acid (4 carbon atoms), making it more polar and hydrophilic (water-attracting). It is less compatible with non-polar organic solvents.Organic solvents, such as hexane, chloroform, or ether, are non-polar and cannot form hydrogen bonds with butyric acid. As a result, butyric acid is less soluble in these solvents.In contrast, palmitic acid's non-polar nature allows it to dissolve more easily in organic solvents, making it more soluble.This difference in solubility is due to the varying degrees of polarity and hydrophobicity between the two fatty acids.

This option suggests that palmitic acid is much more soluble in an organic solvent than oleic acid. Palmitic acid is a saturated fatty acid with 16 carbon atoms, while oleic acid is an unsaturated fatty acid with 18 carbon atoms and one double bond. Generally, saturated fatty acids have lower solubility in organic solvents compared to unsaturated fatty acids.

questions.explanation: Correct option: C) Palmitic acid is much more soluble in an organic solvent than butyric acid.

Acetic acid is the smallest fatty acid. It is a simple carboxylic acid with 2 carbon atoms. Acetic acid is also known as ethanoic acid. You can find an image illustrating the structure of acetic acid by searching on Google for "acetic acid structure image."

questions.explanation: Correct option: D) Acetic acid is the smallest fatty acid.

The name of the dipeptide is derived from the names of the amino acids, with the suffix "-yl" being added to the name of the first amino acid and the prefix "-yl" being added to the name of the second amino acid. questions.text: The primary structure of proteins is stabilized by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Disulfide bonds are bonds that are formed between two cysteine residues. Disulfide bonds are very strong bonds, and they can help to stabilize the tertiary structure of proteins. However, disulfide bonds do not play a role in stabilizing the primary structure of proteins.

Hydrogen bonds are weak bonds that are formed between the hydrogen atom of one molecule and the oxygen atom of another molecule. Hydrogen bonds are important for stabilizing the primary structure of proteins because they help to keep the amino acids in the correct order.

Hydrophobic interactions are interactions that occur between nonpolar groups. Nonpolar groups are groups that do not have any charges. Hydrophobic interactions are important for stabilizing the primary structure of proteins because they help to keep the nonpolar amino acids in the interior of the protein, away from water.

The primary structure of proteins is stabilized by peptide bonds. Peptide bonds are the bonds that link amino acids together to form a polypeptide chain. Peptide bonds are formed when the carboxyl group of one amino acid reacts with the amino group of another amino acid. The reaction releases a molecule of water and forms a covalent bond between the two amino acids.

questions.text: If one amino acid out of 574 amino acids is replaced by another in a hemoglobin molecule it will lose following properties, EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Shape: The shape of hemoglobin is determined by the amino acid sequence of the protein. The replacement of a single amino acid is unlikely to change the overall shape of the protein.

Functional capacity: The functional capacity of hemoglobin is determined by its ability to bind oxygen. The replacement of a single amino acid is unlikely to affect the ability of hemoglobin to bind oxygen.

Oxygen-carrying capacity: The oxygen-carrying capacity of hemoglobin is determined by the number of oxygen molecules that can bind to the protein. The replacement of a single amino acid is unlikely to affect the number of oxygen molecules that can bind to hemoglobin.

If one amino acid out of 574 amino acids is replaced by another in a hemoglobin molecule, it will lose its quaternary level. The quaternary level of a protein refers to the association of multiple protein subunits to form a multimeric protein. Hemoglobin is a quaternary protein, meaning that it is made up of four subunits. The quaternary structure of hemoglobin is important for its ability to bind oxygen.

questions.explanation: By changing sequence of amino acids in a quaternary protein the structure and function of proteins is changed but it will remain a new protein of quaternary level. options.explanation: Ionic bonds can help to stabilize the tertiary structure of proteins by forming between oppositely charged amino acids. For example, the protein hemoglobin contains a number of ionic bonds that help to stabilize its tertiary structure.

Hydrogen bonds can help to stabilize the tertiary structure of proteins by forming between hydrogen atoms that are covalently bonded to electronegative atoms, such as oxygen or nitrogen. For example, the protein myoglobin, which is involved in storing oxygen in muscle cells, contains a number of hydrogen bonds that help to stabilize its tertiary structure.

Disulfide bonds can help to stabilize the tertiary structure of proteins by forming between two cysteine residues that are covalently bonded together. Hydrophobic interactions can help to stabilize the tertiary structure of proteins by forming between nonpolar amino acids that are repelled by water. options.explanation: RNA, or ribonucleic acid, is a molecule closely related to DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and plays a crucial role in the transfer of genetic information and protein synthesis in cells. options.explanation: Ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is a type of RNA (ribonucleic acid) that plays a fundamental role in the process of protein synthesis within cells. It is one of the three main types of RNA, along with messenger RNA (mRNA) and transfer RNA (tRNA). rRNA, together with proteins, forms the structure of ribosomes, which are the cellular organelles responsible for protein synthesis.

Messenger RNA (mRNA) is a type of RNA (ribonucleic acid) molecule that plays a crucial role in the process of protein synthesis within cells. It serves as an intermediate carrier of genetic information from DNA in the cell's nucleus to the ribosomes in the cytoplasm, where proteins are synthesized.

Transfer RNA (tRNA) is a type of RNA (ribonucleic acid) molecule that plays a vital role in the process of protein synthesis within cells. tRNA acts as a translator between the nucleotide language of mRNA (messenger RNA) and the amino acid language of proteins. Its primary function is to bring the appropriate amino acids to the ribosome during protein synthesis.

questions.text: All are biological importance of water, EXCEPT:

options.explanation: Water is a polar molecule, meaning it has a slight positive charge on one end (hydrogen side) and a slight negative charge on the other end (oxygen side). This polarity allows water molecules to form hydrogen bonds with each other and with other polar substances. The polar nature of water is biologically important as it influences various properties and interactions in living organisms. Therefore it can not be the correct answer.

Water is often referred to as the universal solvent because it has the ability to dissolve a wide variety of substances. Due to its polarity, water can effectively interact with charged or polar molecules, breaking them apart and enabling them to dissolve. This property is crucial for many biological processes, such as transportation of nutrients and waste, as well as facilitating chemical reactions within living organisms. Hence it is also not the correct answer.

options.text: High specific heat and high heat of vaporization
options.explanation: Water has a high specific heat, which means it can absorb and store a significant amount of heat energy without a large change in temperature. This property allows water to act as a heat buffer. Additionally, water has a high heat of vaporization, meaning it requires a considerable amount of heat energy to change from a liquid to a gas phase. This property enables water to effectively dissipate heat through processes like evaporation and cooling, contributing to temperature regulation in organisms.

The correct answer is heat of vaporization. The heat of vaporization, also known as the enthalpy of vaporization, is the amount of heat energy required to convert a given quantity of a substance from its liquid phase to its gaseous phase at a constant temperature and pressure.

questions.text: The formation of a large molecule from two small molecules with the removal of water is called:

options.explanation: Condensation, also known as a condensation reaction or dehydration synthesis, is the process of joining two smaller molecules together to form a larger molecule. It is a chemical reaction that occurs when two functional groups, such as hydroxyl (-OH) groups combine, resulting in the release of a water molecule.

Hydrolysis is the opposite process of condensation. It is a chemical reaction in which a larger molecule is broken down into two smaller molecules by the addition of a water molecule. In hydrolysis reactions, a covalent bond is cleaved, and the components of the larger molecule gain a hydrogen atom (-H) from the water molecule and a hydroxyl group (-OH) from another molecule.

Dehydration synthesis, also known as a condensation reaction, is the correct term for the process of forming a large molecule from two small molecules with the removal of a water molecule. This process involves the combination of monomers or smaller subunits to form a polymer while eliminating a water molecule.

options.explanation: Carbonic anhydrase (CA) is an enzyme that catalyzes the reversible hydration of carbon dioxide (CO2) to form bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) and protons (H+). This enzyme plays a crucial role in various physiological processes, particularly in the regulation of acid-base balance, transport of gases, and fluid secretion.

Glycogen is a complex carbohydrate and a form of energy storage in animals, including humans. It is often referred to as animal starch because of its similarity to starch, which is the storage form of carbohydrates in plants. Glycogen is primarily stored in the liver and muscles, and it serves as a readily available source of glucose for the body's energy needs. Glycogen is a polysaccharide composed of many glucose molecules linked together in a branched structure. It is a highly branched molecule, which allows for quick and efficient breakdown when the body requires glucose.

Hemoglobin (often spelled as haemoglobin) is a protein found in red blood cells and is essential for the transport of oxygen from the lungs to various tissues and organs in the body. Hemoglobin also plays a crucial role in the transport of carbon dioxide, a waste product of metabolism, from the tissues back to the lungs, where it can be exhaled.Hemoglobin is a tetrameric protein, meaning it is composed of four protein subunits, each of which is known as a globin. In humans, there are four globin chains: two alpha globin chains and two beta globin chains. Each of these globin chains is bound to a heme group.

Insulin is a peptide hormone produced by the pancreas, specifically by beta cells located in the islets of Langerhans. It plays a crucial role in regulating blood sugar (glucose) levels in the body and is essential for maintaining normal metabolism.One of the primary functions of insulin is to regulate blood glucose levels. When you consume carbohydrates, your digestive system breaks them down into glucose, which enters the bloodstream. Elevated blood glucose levels trigger the release of insulin.

options.explanation: In molecular biology, particularly in the context of DNA, "duplex" refers to the double-stranded structure of DNA. DNA consists of two complementary strands that are held together by hydrogen bonds between the complementary base pairs (adenine pairs with thymine, and guanine pairs with cytosine). The double-stranded structure is often referred to as a DNA duplex.

The term "double-stranded structure" commonly refers to the arrangement of two complementary strands of nucleic acids, such as DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) or RNA (ribonucleic acid). In DNA, the double-stranded structure is often depicted as a double helix. The two strands of DNA run in opposite directions and are held together by hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs. The four nucleotide bases in DNA are adenine (A), thymine (T), cytosine (C), and guanine (G). Adenine pairs with thymine, forming two hydrogen bonds, and cytosine pairs with guanine, forming three hydrogen bonds.

A single-stranded structure refers to a molecule that consists of only one chain of nucleotides or monomers, as opposed to the double-stranded structure where two complementary strands are paired together. The context in which "single-stranded" is used often pertains to nucleic acids, such as RNA (ribonucleic acid) or certain types of DNA.

Genetic material refers to the molecules that carry the genetic instructions used in the growth, development, functioning, and reproduction of all known living organisms. The two primary types of genetic material are DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and RNA (ribonucleic acid).

options.explanation: Messenger RNA (mRNA) is a type of RNA (ribonucleic acid) molecule that plays a crucial role in the process of protein synthesis within cells. It serves as an intermediate carrier of genetic information from DNA in the cell's nucleus to the ribosomes in the cytoplasm, where proteins are synthesized.

Transfer RNA (tRNA) is a type of RNA (ribonucleic acid) molecule that plays a vital role in the process of protein synthesis within cells. tRNA acts as a translator between the nucleotide language of mRNA (messenger RNA) and the amino acid language of proteins. Its primary function is to bring the appropriate amino acids to the ribosome during protein synthesis.

Ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is a type of RNA (ribonucleic acid) that plays a fundamental role in the process of protein synthesis within cells. It is one of the three main types of RNA, along with messenger RNA (mRNA) and transfer RNA (tRNA). rRNA, together with proteins, forms the structure of ribosomes, which are the cellular organelles responsible for protein synthesis.

snRNA stands for "small nuclear RNA." These are a class of short RNA molecules found in the cell's nucleus, and they play essential roles in the process of splicing pre-messenger RNA (pre-mRNA) during gene expression.

options.explanation: DNA, or deoxyribonucleic acid, is a molecule that carries the genetic instructions necessary for the growth, development, functioning, and reproduction of all known living organisms and many viruses. It is often described as the "blueprint of life." DNA is a long, double-stranded helical molecule made up of four basic building blocks called nucleotides. The structure of DNA is a double helix, with two long chains (strands) of nucleotides running in opposite directions and connected by hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs. There are four types of nucleotides in DNA, each characterized by a nitrogenous base.

A chromosome is a thread-like structure composed of DNA and proteins that carries genetic information. Chromosomes are found in the nucleus of eukaryotic cells and, in the context of cellular division, also in the nucleoid region of prokaryotic cells. Chromosomes are essential for the transmission of genetic material from one generation of cells or organisms to the next.

A ribosome is a cellular structure that functions as the site of protein synthesis in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. It plays a crucial role in translating genetic information from messenger RNA (mRNA) into functional proteins. Ribosomes are composed of two subunits, and they are typically found in the cytoplasm of the cell.

A nucleosome is a basic structural unit of DNA packaging in eukaryotic cells. It consists of DNA wrapped around a core of histone proteins. Nucleosomes play a fundamental role in organizing and compacting the long strands of DNA, allowing it to fit within the confined space of the cell nucleus.

questions.explanation: A ribosome is a cellular structure that functions as the site of protein synthesis in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. Ribosomes are composed of two subunits, and they are typically found in the cytoplasm of the cell.

options.explanation: Fructose is a monosaccharide, a simple sugar, and it is one of the three most important dietary monosaccharides, along with glucose and galactose. It has the same chemical formula as glucose (C6H12O6), but the arrangement of atoms is different, making it an isomer of glucose. Fructose is commonly found in fruits, honey, and certain vegetables, and it is often referred to as "fruit sugar."

Sucrose is a common disaccharide, meaning it is composed of two monosaccharide sugar units. Specifically, sucrose is made up of one molecule of glucose and one molecule of fructose, joined together by a glycosidic bond. It is often referred to as table sugar and is the most widely used sugar in food and beverage production.

Maltose is a disaccharide composed of two glucose molecules linked together by an α-1,4-glycosidic bond. It is often referred to as malt sugar. Maltose is produced during the germination of certain grains, particularly barley, as part of the breakdown of starch. It is commonly found in malted foods and beverages.

Galactose is a monosaccharide sugar, meaning it is a single sugar unit and cannot be further hydrolyzed into simpler sugars. It is one of the two monosaccharides that make up lactose, the sugar found in milk. The other monosaccharide in lactose is glucose. Galactose can be found in various foods and is an important component of glycolipids and glycoproteins in the body.

It is often referred to as table sugar and is the most widely used sugar in food and beverage production.
